item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty good, but I'm a little confused about the extent of their telepathy. Do they just share memories and thoughts, or do they also share feelings? In other words, if one Eric was punched in the face, would the others all suddenly feel pain? Or, another example, if someone cut one Eric on his left cheek, would a cut suddenly materialize on the left cheeks of all the other Erics? If it's supposed to be like that, I feel like they all should have died when the fifth Eric died in the car accident.

Other than that, I think the only thing this is missing is some kind of creepy, implied origin of how Eric split into five telepathically linked individuals who are unaware of their condition. Like, perhaps a Foundation agent looking through some old newspaper archives finds an article about some mysterious accident affecting an area where a young couple lives with their newborn child, and it's reported that the child survived with only minor injuries that didn't require hospitalization, and the agent notices that it's the same address Eric's family lived at when he was born, at the time they were living there, puts two and two together, and includes a heavily redacted copy of the article in the file.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations. This is my favorite SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The government sees one social security number, filing five copies of the same return each year.
Those five returns each claim the same five incomes and the same five sets of dependents.
Now, I'm no accountant, but I'd think that should set off some sort of alarms at the IRS.

Especially since he seems well-off x5, I'd think the IRS would start suspecting some sort of bizarre attempt at fraud or identity theft and break out the really big magnifying glass reserved for special audits.

Never mind The Foundation, the mundane authorities should have uncovered this skip years before the suicide attempt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm binging Smapti skips rn and I stumble upon this masterpiece. It's amazingly bizarre, and I laughed a little bit on more than one occasion. This is excellent.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, This is now one of my favorite SCiPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this so freaking much. I'll even conciously ignore the plotholes. Upvoted hard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Counting the deceased Eric, would you say that these ARE your five favourite SCPs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of gta5 a lot more than it should seeing as theyre not /that/ similar. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How could this guy stay uncontained for so long? How did this guy manage to be a successful lawyer, small business owner, etc. while speaking like an alien and expecting his co-workers and employees to literally do four things at once?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I missed this one, its definitely now in my top five favourite SCPs.

item.title: Agreement,
item.content:encoded:
But - in some cases the seatbelt is not properly connected in the first place. It gets jammed in kinda sideways, but doesn't "click" and become properly fastened.

So, yeah, not really that strange. Especially with older cars, or a driver in a hurry.

:)

(Had to add this because I am such a clutz that this honestly does happen to me. :) )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Although one thing bugged me. It is VERY hard for a seatbelt to release in an accident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to say how wonderfully surreal I found this peice. A great example of "weird" without creepy. +1

item.title: Re: Oh dear...
item.content:encoded: It was Bacon, Oven, and Fry, right?

item.title: Re: Oh dear...
item.content:encoded: Holy crap. Multiple Erics aside, this is so totally Wayside logic. Five bedrooms, just like everybody…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way the mother apparently laughed at "his" complaints about "his brothers" suggests that she knows what's going on.

They had one hell of a nice mum; having one crazy kid who eats like 5? Not many would have been that kind.

item.title: Oh dear...
item.content:encoded: it's Wayside School all over again…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a better picture. They're not clawing each other's throats out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Entertaining, wonderfully odd, and I want to know more about poor Mr. Erics. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't buy the linguistic contortions at first, but as the interviews went on I was won over. Quirky and a tad creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. There are logic gaps you can drive a truck through, but I don't care. +1

Thirding all this. Sometimes something truly entertaining can stretch suspension of disbelief well past the breaking point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm too lazy to look it up, but I think there's another SCP where two siblings share memories, and one has the left half of the brain while the other has the right half.

I like this memory-sharing SCP so much more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought of that too, although this seems to be one step further. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I skipped the interviews a while back because I was busy, but this is positively delightful! It has a weirdness and joy mixed with sinister-ness that really grabs one. Especially the way he talks about "Everybody dies for the first time" and "I had five bedrooms, just like anyone" really make you think about a different way of living…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think that was kind of what I was trying to say. This isn't an "Eric" article, it is about a persons who is called Eric. I personally don't think it needs to be changed, but I was just trying to explain why some people were confused.

That said, again, I really like this article! I agree that you don't really need to change anything if you don't want to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. There are logic gaps you can drive a truck through, but I don't care. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, it would be a sharp change in tone but it's still fun to think about, especially if the interviewed subject was mad that they were torturing him while asking him stupid questions about those other jerks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had started to write a fourth interview pretty similar to this. The idea was that the doctor would be questioning one of them while an agent in another room held a gun to a second one's head. The first one would be shown video of the second one on a monitor - the idea being to try to shock him into realizing there's more than one of him.

I decided it was too abrupt a tone change from the rest of what i'd written and I chose not to put it in the finished article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One question, if you tortured one of these guys would the others also be tortured? what would an interview be like with the one of the others while the torture was happening? what would the one being tortured even say since he's being tortured while sitting at a desk and undergoing an interview at the same time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The plot holes don't bother me at all, not compared to the mind- bending weirdness of the concept. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having now read the "Eric" SCPs, I can say that this is in no way meant to be connected to them. Seeing as this article has hit +53 in under 24 hours, I'm going to assume it's a minor enough coincidence that it doesn't need to be changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he and Tanhony are part of the same machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but make the "Misaka-Misaka" reference in my head when I read this article. Still, I like concept. Always been very interesting to me.

If you don't know what "Misaka-Misaka" is, google it. It's pretty much free-lols, with a side of heartbreak.

This gets a +1 from me. And least we're not sharing the same ideas this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti you're an animal.

And by animal I mean robot, at this rate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderfully weird. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is nothing about this I do not love.

"There were five of them once" was the "oh shit" moment for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, you mean Eric isn't an SCP himself? This makes me sad.

"Can I see my calculators now? I haven't used those since middle school!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like this more if it wasn't Eric, because I already have one in my headcanon. That's actually bothering me so much I can't bring myself to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah I see your problem trying to work it into the story.

Regarding their parents; I'm willing to buy into that, those parents could have made themselves believe their kids were just like others, and choose not to notice the weird anomalous behavior. It's what some parents do right?

But the government not noticing anything doesn't fly with me.. Or even employers; what the hell would their CV's look like?
I know, it's pain in the ass to to come up with a solution for that.
Regardless, I gave it my upvote, because it really makes you think about how those guys go through life, and what it would be like to be like them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very weird article. I like it. The way they interact and act as if it totally normal to have five sets of memories at once is I think what makes this work.

As for Eric, it is kind of an easter egg in some articles about sentient toy-like SCP's, like the calculator and the ball of yarn. The implication is that there was some kid or person called Eric who had a whole bunch of SCPs for companions when he was young that were abandoned or found by the Foundation. Check the "eric" page tags. This article doesn't fit with the general idea of the other Eric articles, but it's not like Eric is a totally uncommon name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.scp-wiki.net/system:page-tags/tag/eric#pages

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll grant that it could be seen as a plot hole that nobody caught on until these guys were in their 30s, but I don't see a way to plug it without substantially reworking the story - the Foundation would have taken these guys in much earlier if they were identified as teenagers. I don't particularly want to backdate the story to the pre-information age either - I personally prefer for SCPs to be written in a tone of "recent timelessness" that implies that it's up to date as of now or of a few years ago, and changing the date from 20██ to 19██ would throw that off.

Unless I come up with a better way to justify it in-story, my current understanding is that their parents either didn't just thought "Our kids sure are weird, aren't they?" and didn't put too much thought into the way they acted, or actively encouraged the belief that they were just normal people. School officials didn't look too closely at their documents when they were enrolled in school, and for some reason no government person ever made the connection that this one person was doing far more than should have been possible for an individual to do.

Re: the picture - turns out there aren't many good photos of identical quadruplets that fit the parameters of the article I was writing. I think it can be assumed, since this was taken just after they were all arrested and trying to commit suicide, that they were ordered to pose for a photo for archive purposes, and were probably a little disoriented.

I'm still really not sure what the whole Eric thing is about - I could easily change it if it's too much of a distraction, but it doesn't seem to be a huge problem so i'm leaving it as is for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw the picture, sighed a bit thinking about some type of human who could clone himself.

Then i read the article, and my hope for humanity was restored.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how well that picture works; as much as they're described hating each other, I can't see them calmly sitting on a bench to take a picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is insane and awesome and hits home for me because I've frequently wished to have exactly this ability

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. And I'm obviously missing something because I'm not getting the whole "Eric connection" thing either.

That said, this makes perfect sense that these guys still can't quite wrap their heads around the fact that not everyone lives five lives simultaneously; that's kinda how (some) psychological conditions work. Even with reality smacking you in the face, your mind goes through a bizarre array of gymnastics to justify its own perceptions.

That said, yes I think *somehow* there would have been news of this, especially given the fact that there was a single birth certificate, but somewhere between there and school the one Eric split into five. Unless… Unless mom was in on it. Hmmm…. *dons conspiracy hat, begins smoking Sherlock Holmes pipe*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So set this in the early/mid-90s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guys, guys. People can be named Eric without being the Eric.

Also, this is a weak concept fairly well executed, but it does get a bit (deliberately) confusing with the interviews. I'm pretty sure there's a logical hole somewhere in them, but I don't know what it is yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or in this case, there would have to be some kind of trail of evidence. The mother would have to have had these kids in some kind of therapy well before high school. Neighbors who noticed weird things would post about it online or talk about it to their friends. And I do imagine the Foundation has some kind of feelers out somewhere that would pick up on any one of the various pieces of anomalous evidence and begin investigating.

Either way, I like it; it just seems like the background has some holes in it. I can ignore them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I don't think he meant to contribute tothe whole Eric work.

That being said, I do actually like this a bit tied in with the whole Eric thing.

I really like this article a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with vez. There's a lot of crazy theories about Eric, and I may be biased here since I've contributed to that particular body of work, but this does not feel like it fits at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing about computers, though… makes it easy to pick up anomalies like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… you didn't know about "Eric" when you chose that name for this SCP?

Oh my. That's just perfect.

It's like… okay, all the Simpsons are named after members of Matt Groening's immediate family — his parents are Marge and Homer, and he has two younger sisters named Lisa and Maggie. And then, when it came time to create a first name for Grampa Simpson, the writers asked Groening "what's your grandfather's name?"

and Groening said "No. No, I'm not going to do this again. I'm not going to give you his name. I'm going to leave the room, and you guys will come up with a first name for Grampa Simpson independent of me, and independent of my actual grandfather. Okay?"

so they said okay, and Groening left the room.

And ten minutes later, he came back in, and they said "okay, Grampa Simpson's first name is 'Abraham'."

and Matt Groening said "holy shit."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep hearing John Cleese asking for a fish license. But that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not aware of an "Eric connection". I picked an Anglo-American first name at random - it could have just as easily been Bill, or John, or Justin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is crazy as hell, but I don't like the Eric connection. Still, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless the Foundation has embedded resources working undercover as high school dance chaperones now, I think it's pretty unlikely that quintuplets fighting over girls, however rare that scenario might be, would be significant enough to warrant their attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One question: How has this never been discovered before? How did five insane identical siblings attend a high school, behave like that, and the Foundation didn't pick them up then?
